title: Hearing - Sales and/or Use Tax (State,Local,MTA) - 202602010H
text:
SOAH DOCKET NO. 304-25-16547

CPA HEARING NO. 120,565

 


RE:      **************


TAXPAYER NO:  **************

AUDIT OFFICE:   **************

AUDIT PERIOD:   December 1, 2023 THROUGH December 31, 2023


Sales And Use Tax/RFD

BEFORE THE COMPTROLLER OF PUBLIC ACCOUNTS

OF THE STATE OF TEXAS


KELLY HANCOCK
Acting Texas Comptroller of Public Accounts


TERESA GUTIERREZ SMITH

Representing Respondent


**************

Representing Claimant


 



COMPTROLLER’S DECISION
 

This decision is considered final on March 2, 2026, unless a motion for rehearing is timely filed; this date of finality is calculated based on the Administrative Procedure Act (APA).[1]  The failure to timely file a motion for rehearing may result in adverse legal consequences.


Administrative Law Judge (ALJ) Kathy Pickup of the State Office of Administrative Hearings (SOAH) issued a Proposal for Decision (PFD) that includes Findings of Fact and Conclusions of Law.  SOAH served the PFD on each party and each party was given an opportunity to file exceptions and replies with SOAH in accordance with SOAH’s rules of procedure.  The ALJ recommended that the Comptroller adopt the PFD as written.


After review and consideration, IT IS ORDERED that the PFD is adopted as changed.[2] 


The result from this Decision is Attachment A.  The ALJ’s recommendation letter is Attachment B.  The PFD as changed is Attachment C.  Attachments A, B, and C are incorporated by reference.


Attachment A reflects a zero amount due.

 
SIGNED on this 5th day of February 2026

 

KELLY HANCOCK
Acting Comptroller of Public Accounts


By: Lisa Craven

Deputy Comptroller


Attachment A, Texas Notification of Hearing Results
Attachment B, ALJ’s recommendation letter
Attachment C, Proposal for Decision as changed

 

 

 


ATTACHMENT C

 

 

SOAH Docket No. 304-25-16547                                                             

Before the State Office of Administrative Hearings

TCPA Hearing No. 120,565

 

Proposal for Decision

 

************** (Claimant) submitted a claim asking the Texas Comptroller of Public Accounts (Comptroller) to refund sales tax that it alleges was erroneously remitted. The Comptroller’s Tax Division (Staff) denied the claim in full. Claimant requested a refund hearing, contending it never received the money reflected on the invoices. Staff argues that Claimant has not met its burden of proof. In this Proposal for Decision, the Administrative Law Judge (ALJ) recommends denying the refund claim.

 

I. Notice, Jurisdiction, and Procedural History

 

Staff referred the contested case to the State Office of Administrative Hearings (SOAH) and, on April 14, 2025, ALJ Kathy Pickup issued Order Scheduling Hearing on the Merits and Setting Pre-Hearing Requirements, which included the requirements for the oral hearing. On April 15, 2025, Staff issued a Notice of Hearing to Claimant. The hearing was continued and convened in person on October 29, 2025. Teresa Gutierrez Smith represented Staff, and Claimant was represented by its tax and compliance manager, **************. The record close date was extended to November 3, 2025, to allow Claimant to file an Exhibit List and Exhibits.

 

There are no issues of notice or jurisdiction; therefore, those matters are set out in the Findings of Fact and Conclusions of Law without further discussion.

 

II. Reasons for Decision

 

A. Evidence

 

Staff presented the testimony of Miguel Zaldivar, auditor, and offered the Hearing Request Receipt as its Exhibit 1.

 

Claimant presented the testimony of ************** and submitted the following exhibits:


	
	February 6, 2024 Letter from Comptroller to Claimant;
	
	
	March 20, 2024 Letter from Comptroller to Claimant;
	
	
	March 12, 2024 Email from Claimant to Comptroller;
	
	
	Invoices;
	
	
	Credit Invoices;
	
	
	List of Journal Entry Numbers;
	
	
	March 13, 2024 Email from Comptroller to Claimant. [ENDNOTE 3]
	


 

Staff’s exhibit was admitted without objection. Staff objected to Claimant’s exhibits as they were not provided to Staff prior to the hearing. The auditor indicated that he would be able to review Claimant’s exhibits and be able to testify about them if we took a brief recess. We took a brief recess, and the auditor reviewed Claimant’s exhibits. Therefore, the objection was overruled, and Claimant’s exhibits were admitted to the record.

 

B. Agreed Adjustments

 

Staff did not agree to any refunds.

 

C. Facts Established and Issues Presented

 

During the period at issue, December 1, 2023, through December 31, 2023, Claimant operated a software business selling Software as a Service (SaaS). Mr. ************** stated that Claimant is on the accrual accounting system. On March 12, 2024, Claimant filed a refund claim contending that it erroneously remitted sales tax on customer invoices that had not been paid. There is no dispute that Claimant operated a software business selling SaaS or that SaaS is a taxable service. On March 13, 2024, the Comptroller’s Revenue Accounting Division asked Claimant to provide its 11-digit Texas taxpayer number, refund amount, documentation, and reason for the request. Claimant did not provide the requested information. Staff denied the claim in full. Claimant requested a refund hearing, and Staff referred the matter to SOAH.

 

Claimant provided invoices, credit invoices, and other summary information at the hearing. After a recess to allow the auditor to review the new documentation, Staff maintained its position that a refund was not warranted.

 

D. ALJ Analysis and Recommendation

 

Texas imposes a tax on each sale of a taxable item in this state. Tex. Tax Code § 151.051. The term “taxable item” includes tangible personal property and taxable services. Id. § 151.010. When tax is imposed on tangible personal property the taxing entity’s prima facie burden of proof is easily met because, unless an exemption applies, all sales of tangible personal property in this state are taxable. Id.; see also, Comptroller’s Decision No. 117,129 (2022). However, only services specifically enumerated in Texas Tax Code § 151.0101 are taxable. Therefore, to impose tax on a service, Staff must demonstrate not only that a service was sold but that the service is taxable. Id. If that burden is satisfied, Claimant must demonstrate by a preponderance of the evidence that that tax was paid, accrued, or assessed on a service in error. See 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(e).

 

The law requires retailers to keep records that reflect the total gross receipts from sales and the total purchases of taxable items. Tex. Tax Code § 151.025; 34 Tex. Admin Code § 3.281(b). In a contested case hearing, the taxpayer must produce sufficient records and supporting documentation appropriate to the tax or fee for the transactions in question to substantiate and enable verification of the taxpayer’s claim related to the amount of tax, penalty, or interest to be assessed, collected, or refunded. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(a). A taxpayer is generally required to support its contentions with source records, and assertions in pleadings are insufficient to meet the burden of proof. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 105,892 (2012). Summary documents that are created by the taxpayer and submitted without underlying support are tantamount to bare assertions, i.e., they are not considered evidence. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision Nos. 115,535 (2020), 102,488 (2010).

 

Data processing is a taxable service.  Tex. Tax Code §§  151.0035, .0101(a)(12). A taxable data processing service “includes word processing, data entry, data retrieval, data search, information compilation, payroll and business accounting data production, and other computerized data and information storage or manipulation.” Tex. Tax Code §§ 151.0101(a)(12); .0035. The use of the term “includes” in Section 151.0035 demonstrates a legislative intent to define data processing as broader than the enumerated activities stated in the statute. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision Nos. 105,009 (2012); 47,246 (2007). The Comptroller has determined that data processing service providers include sellers of SaaS and application service providers. See Comptroller Publication No. 96-259, Taxable Services (July 2022).

 

If the Comptroller finds that an amount of tax, penalty, or interest has been unlawfully or erroneously collected, the Comptroller shall credit or refund the amount. Tex. Tax Code § 111.104(a). When a taxpayer requests a refund, it must establish by a preponderance of evidence that taxes were erroneously collected or paid. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 109,787 (2015); 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(e).

 

For sales and use tax purposes, retailers may use a cash basis, an accrual basis, or any generally recognized accounting basis that correctly reflects the operation of their business. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.302(b)(a)(1). Claimant asserts that it uses the accrual basis accounting method. If Claimant is an accrual taxpayer, it may take a credit or receive a refund for bad debt. Any portion of the sales price of a taxable item that the retailer or private label credit provider cannot collect is considered to be a bad debt. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.302(a)(3)(d). A retailer is not required to report tax on any amount entered in the retailer’s books as a bad debt during the reporting period in which the sale was made, and that will be taken as a deduction on the federal income tax return during the same or subsequent reporting period. Id. § 3.302(d)(1). Additionally, a retailer is entitled to a credit for tax reported and paid on an account later determined to be a bad debt. Id. § 3.302(d)(2). The records of the person who claims the bad debt deduction must show:

 

(i) date of original credit sale and name and Texas sales and use tax permit number of the retailer who collected and remitted the sales and use tax to the comptroller;

(ii) amount that the purchaser contracted to pay;

(iii) taxable and nontaxable charges;

(iv) all other payments or other credits applied to the account of the purchaser;

(v) evidence that the uncollected amount has been designated as a bad debt in the books and records of the person who claims the bad debt deduction, and that the amount has been claimed as a bad debt deduction for federal income tax purposes;

(vi) identification of each city, county, transit authority, or special purpose district to which local taxes were reported if the claimant is claiming a refund or credit of local taxes;

(vii) the sales and use tax collected and remitted to the comptroller; and

(viii) any additional records requested by the comptroller to verify a credit or refund claim.

 

A. date of original sale and name and Texas sales tax permit number of the retailer;

B. name and address of purchaser;

C. amount that the purchaser contracted to pay;

D. taxable and nontaxable charges;

E. amount on which the retailer reported and paid Texas tax;

F. all payments or other credits applied to the account of the purchaser;

G. evidence that the uncollected amount has been designated as a bad debt in the books and records of the person who claims the bad debt deduction, and that the amount has been or will be claimed as a bad debt deduction for income tax purposes;

H. city, county, transit authority, or special purpose district to which local taxes were reported; and

I. the unpaid portion of the assigned sales price.

 

Id. § 3.302(d)(7)(C).

 

Source: See Conclusion of Law No. 25.

 

Claimant stated that it was not claiming the refund as bad debt but did not explain an alternative basis on which it was claiming the refund. Claimant did not have the evidence necessary to establish a bad debt claim. Therefore, to the extent Claimant is an accrual taxpayer, the ALJ recommends denying its refund claim.

 

If Claimant used the cash basis, which contradicts Mr. **************’s statement, then the claim is not for a bad debt deduction, and proof that the unpaid invoices were claimed as bad debts for federal tax purposes is not required. However, Claimant must prove by a preponderance of the evidence that it did not receive the payments pursuant to the contracts and invoices and reflected in the sales and use tax returns. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(e). The evidence submitted includes the invoices, credit invoices, and internally produced summary documents created by the taxpayer. Claimant contends this is evidence that the invoices were not paid. However, Claimant’s evidence is insufficient. A person is presumed to have received or collected a tax or money represented to be a tax for the purpose of this section if the person files, or causes to be filed, a tax return or report with the comptroller showing tax due. Tex. Tax Code § 111.016(a-1). In the absence of collection letters and other evidence that the invoices remain unpaid, Claimant’s documents are not accepted as establishing its contention by a preponderance of the evidence. Claimant’s refund claim should remain denied.

 

III. Findings of Fact


	
	During the period at issue, December 1, 2023, through December 31, 2023, ************** (Claimant) operated a software business selling Software as a Service (SaaS).
	
	
	Claimant was on the accrual accounting system during the period at issue.
	
	
	On March 12, 2024, Claimant filed a refund claim contending that it remitted sales tax on customer invoices that had not been paid.
	
	
	On March 13, 2024, the Comptroller’s Revenue Accounting Division asked Claimant to provide its 11-digit Texas taxpayer number, refund amount, documentation, and reason for the request. Claimant did not provide the requested information.
	
	
	The Tax Division (Staff) of the Texas Comptroller of Public Accounts (Comptroller) denied the claim in full.
	
	
	Claimant requested a refund hearing, and Staff referred the matter to the State Office of Administrative Hearings (SOAH).
	
	
	On April 14, 2025, Administrative Law Judge (ALJ) Kathy Pickup issued Order Scheduling Hearing on the Merits and Setting Pre-Hearing Requirements, which included the requirements for the oral hearing.
	
	
	On April 15, 2025, Staff issued a Notice of Hearing to Claimant. The notice contained the location, date, and time of the hearing; a statement of the nature of the hearing; a statement of the legal authority and jurisdiction under which the hearing was to be held; a reference to the particular sections of the statutes and rules involved; and a short, plain statement of the factual matters asserted, or an attachment that incorporated by reference the factual matters asserted in the complaint or petition filed with the state agency.
	
	
	The hearing was continued and convened in person on October 29, 2025.
	
	
	Claimant provided invoices, credit invoices, and other summary information at the hearing. After a recess to allow the auditor to review the new documentation, Staff maintained its position that a refund was not warranted.
	
	
	At the conclusion of the hearing, the ALJ extended the record close date and ordered Claimant to file an Exhibit List with numbered exhibits.
	
	
	The record closed November 3, 2025.
	


 

IV. Conclusions of Law


	
	The Comptroller has jurisdiction over this matter pursuant to Texas Tax Code ch. 111.
	
	
	SOAH has jurisdiction over matters related to this hearing, including the authority to issue a proposal for decision with findings of fact and conclusions of law pursuant to Texas Government Code ch. 2003.
	
	
	Staff provided proper and timely notice of the hearing pursuant to Texas Government Code ch. 2001 and Texas Tax Code § 111.105.
	
	
	Texas imposes a tax on each sale of a taxable item in this state. Tex. Tax Code § 151.051.
	
	
	The term “taxable item” includes tangible personal property and taxable services. Tex. Tax Code § 151.010.
	
	
	When tax is imposed on tangible personal property the taxing entity’s prima facie burden of proof is easily met because, unless an exemption applies, all sales of tangible personal property in this state are taxable. Tex. Tax Code § 151.010; see also, Comptroller’s Decision No. 117,129 (2022).
	
	
	Only services specifically enumerated in Texas Tax Code § 151.0101 are taxable.
	
	
	To impose tax on a service, Staff must demonstrate not only that a service was sold but that the service is taxable. Tex. Tax Code § 151.010.
	
	
	If that burden is satisfied, Claimant must demonstrate by a preponderance of the evidence that that tax was paid, accrued, or assessed on a service in error. See 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(e). 151.010.
	
	
	The law requires retailers to keep records that reflect the total gross receipts from sales and the total purchases of taxable items. Tex. Tax Code § 151.025; 34 Tex. Admin Code § 3.281(b).
	
	
	In a contested case hearing, the taxpayer must produce sufficient records and supporting documentation appropriate to the tax or fee for the transactions in question to substantiate and enable verification of the taxpayer’s claim related to the amount of tax, penalty, or interest to be assessed, collected, or refunded. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(a).
	
	
	A taxpayer is generally required to support its contentions with source records, and assertions in pleadings are insufficient to meet the burden of proof. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 105,892 (2012).
	
	
	Summary documents that are created by the taxpayer and submitted without underlying support are tantamount to bare assertions, i.e., they are not considered evidence. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision Nos. 115,535 (2020), 102,488 (2010).
	
	
	Data processing is a taxable service.  Tex. Tax Code §§ 151.0035, .0101(a)(12).
	
	
	A taxable data processing service “includes word processing, data entry, data retrieval, data search, information compilation, payroll and business accounting data production, and other computerized data and information storage or manipulation.” Tex. Tax Code §§ 151.0101(a)(12); .0035.
	
	
	The use of the term “includes” in Section 151.0035 demonstrates a legislative intent to define data processing as broader than the enumerated activities stated in the statute. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision Nos. 105,009 (2012); 47,246 (2007).
	
	
	The Comptroller has determined that data processing service providers include sellers of SaaS and application service providers. See Comptroller Publication No. 96-259, Taxable Services, July 2022.
	
	
	If the Comptroller finds that an amount of tax, penalty, or interest has been unlawfully or erroneously collected, the Comptroller shall credit or refund the amount. Tex. Tax Code § 111.104(a).
	
	
	When a taxpayer requests a refund, it must establish by a preponderance of evidence that taxes were erroneously collected or paid. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 109,787 (2015); 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(e).
	
	
	For sales and use tax purposes, retailers may use a cash basis, an accrual basis, or any generally recognized accounting basis that correctly reflects the operation of their business. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.302(b)(a)(1).
	
	
	If Claimant is an accrual taxpayer, it may take a credit or receive a refund for bad debt.
	
	
	Any portion of the sales price of a taxable item that the retailer or private label credit provider cannot collect is considered to be a bad debt. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.302(a)(3)(d).
	
	
	A retailer is not required to report tax on any amount that has been entered in the retailer’s books as a bad debt during the reporting period in which the sale was made, and that will be taken as a deduction on the federal income tax return during the same or subsequent reporting period. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.302(d).
	
	
	A retailer is entitled to a credit for tax reported and paid on an account later determined to be a bad debt. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.302(d).
	
	
	The records of the person who claims the bad debt deduction must show: date of original credit sale and name and Texas sales and use tax permit number of the retailer who collected and remitted the sales and use tax to the comptroller; amount that the purchaser contracted to pay; taxable and nontaxable charges; all other payments or other credits applied to the account of the purchaser; evidence that the uncollected amount has been designated as a bad debt in the books and records of the person who claims the bad debt deduction, and that the amount has been claimed as a bad debt deduction for federal income tax purposes; identification of each city, county, transit authority, or special purpose district to which local taxes were reported if the claimant is claiming a refund or credit of local taxes; the sales and use tax collected and remitted to the comptroller; and  any additional records requested by the comptroller to verify a credit or refund claim. date of original sale and name and Texas sales tax permit number of the retailer; name and address of purchaser; amount that the purchaser contracted to pay; taxable and nontaxable charges; amount on which the retailer reported and paid Texas tax; all payments or other credits applied to the account of the purchaser; evidence that the uncollected amount has been designated as a bad debt in the books and records of the person who claims the bad debt deduction, and that the amount has been or will be claimed as a bad debt deduction for income tax purposes; city, county, transit authority, or special purpose district to which local taxes were reported; and the unpaid portion of the assigned sales price. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.302(d).
	


Source: For Conclusions of Law Nos. 20, 22, and 25, see 46 TexReg 9410 (effective January 6, 2022); see also, Finding of Fact No. 1 (the period at issue is December 1, 2023, through December 31, 2023).


	
	If Claimant is an accrual taxpayer, Claimant did not show by a preponderance of the evidence that it is entitled to a refund.
	
	
	If Claimant used the cash basis then the claim is not for a bad debt deduction, and proof that the unpaid invoices were claimed as bad debts for federal tax purposes is not required. However, Claimant must prove by a preponderance of the evidence that it did not receive the payments pursuant to the contracts and invoices and reflected in the sales and use tax returns. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(e).
	
	
	A person is presumed to have received or collected a tax or money represented to be a tax for the purpose of this section if the person files, or causes to be filed, a tax return or report with the comptroller showing tax due. Tex. Tax Code § 111.016(a-1).
	
	
	If Claimant is on the cash basis, Claimant did not show by a preponderance of the evidence that it is entitled to a refund.
	
	
	Claimant’s refund claim should remain denied.
	


 

Signed November 4, 2025

 

Kathy Pickup

Presiding Administrative Law Judge

 

 

 




ENDNOTES



[1] The date calculated is 25 days after this decision is signed. See APA, Tex. Gov’t Code § 2001.146(a); S.B. 1095, Acts 2017, 85th Leg.   For additional guidance, refer to the Frequently Asked Questions Related to Motions for Rehearing, found here: http://comptroller.texas.gov/taxes/publications/96-1789.pdf



[2] See Tex. Gov’t Code § 2003.101(e) and (f). 

[3] At the hearing, Claimant also presented an Excel spreadsheet as part of its exhibits, which was admitted to the record. The Excel spreadsheet consisted of Claimant’s Exhibits 4, 5, and 6. When Claimant filed its exhibits, the spreadsheet was not listed on the Exhibit List or contained in the exhibits Claimant filed. The ALJ therefore considers that exhibit withdrawn.
subjects: BAD DEBTS-ACCRUAL BASIS OF ACCOUNTING VS. CASH BASIS
BARE ASSERTIONS IN PLEADINGS ARE INSUFFICIENT TO MEET THE BURDEN OF PROOF-MUST BE SUPPORTED BY BUSINESS RECORDS
